content.problemStatement:
JJ has an array AAA. He can perform the following operation on AAA:


Divide AAA into two subsequences PPP and QQQ such that each AiA_iAi​ belongs to either PPP or QQQ.
Set A:=P concat QA := P\ \texttt{concat}\ QA:=P concat Q
Here concat\texttt{concat}concat denotes the concatenation operation. For e.g. [2,1,5] concat [4,3]=[2,1,5,4,3][2, 1, 5] \texttt{ concat } [4, 3] = [2, 1, 5, 4, 3][2,1,5] concat [4,3]=[2,1,5,4,3].

Is it possible to make AAA sorted in non-decreasing order after applying the above operation **at most once**?

Note: An array XXX is a subsequence of an array YYY if XXX can be obtained by deletion of several (possibly, zero or all) elements from YYY.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if it is possible to make the array AAA sorted after applying the given operation **at most once**. Otherwise, output `NO`.


You may print each character of `YES` and `NO` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs`, `Yes` will be considered identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N (the size of the array). The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing YES if the array can be sorted in non‑decreasing order after applying the described operation at most once, otherwise output NO.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: choose P=[1,2,3] and Q=[4,5,6] → [1,2,3,4,5,6].\nTest case 2: no split can produce a sorted array.\nTest case 3: choose P=[5,7,9] and Q=[10,11] → [5,7,9,10,11].

solution.approach: Let B be the sorted version of A. We try to build B by first taking a subsequence P that will become the prefix of the final array. Scan A from left to right with a pointer j on B. If the current element equals B[j] we assign it to P and increment j; otherwise we put the element into a list R (the elements that will belong to Q). After the scan, the first j elements of B have been matched by P. The operation succeeds iff the remaining list R is exactly the suffix B[j…N‑1]. This works because any feasible solution must use a prefix of the sorted array as P, and taking the earliest possible matches never harms feasibility. The algorithm runs in O(N log N) per test case for sorting and O(N) extra work.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (dominated by sorting)
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the sorted copy and the residual list

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data=list(map(int,sys.stdin.read().split()))
    it=iter(data)
    t=next(it)
    out=[]
    for _ in range(t):
        n=next(it)
        a=[next(it) for __ in range(n)]
        b=sorted(a)
        j=0
        rem=[]
        for x in a:
            if j<n and x==b[j]:
                j+=1
            else:
                rem.append(x)
        ok=j+len(rem)==n and rem==b[j:]
        out.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))
if __name__=='__main__':
    solve()
